food_subgroup: Non-alcoholic fruit and herb infused beverages

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mint agua fresca. FOODON_03301710 (fruit drink) is the closest parent category covering lightly sweetened herb-and-fruit infused water beverages.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for agua fresca. AN000 (water-based beverages, non-alcoholic) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to mint agua fresca or agua fresca as a beverage category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (one glass), which is typical for a restaurant-served agua fresca in a Mexican dining context.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, water, tap, drinking — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for mint-infused water and sugar-sweetened beverages for estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mint agua fresca is not a standardised entry in any primary regional database (HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for water (FDC ID 175739), fresh mint leaves (FDC ID 173474), lime juice (FDC ID 167747), and granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655), combined in proportions typical of a standard agua fresca recipe (approximately 90% water, 5% sugar, 2% lime juice, 3% mint infusion). Per-100g values reflect the dilute, lightly sweetened nature of the beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mint agua fresca. GI estimated at 40 based on analogy with lightly sweetened fruit-infused water and dilute sugar-water beverages (sucrose solution ~65 diluted by ~90% water content reduces effective GI; lemonade and dilute fruit drinks in the University of Sydney GI database range from 30–54). A GI of 40 is a conservative mid-range estimate for a lightly sweetened beverage (~4.5g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly sucrose). Glycemic load per 300g serving = (40 × 13.5g carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.4, rounded to 6.1 accounting for lime juice fructose contribution. gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg per 100g; ~9 mg per 300 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Mint agua fresca is very low in sodium (~9 mg per standard serving) and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension, making it a suitable beverage choice when prepared without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~4.3g sugar per 100g; ~13g per 300 ml serving) contributing to glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the added sugar in mint agua fresca (~13g per standard 300 ml serving, GL ~6), and may consider reducing sugar or substituting a non-nutritive sweetener to lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; low sodium; mint provides minor antioxidant contribution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mint agua fresca contains negligible saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium, and is generally compatible with a heart-healthy diet, though regular consumption of the sweetened version should be moderated to limit added sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mint Agua Fresca (Agua Fresca de Menta) is a traditional Mexican cold beverage made by infusing fresh mint leaves in water, typically sweetened with sugar and brightened with lime juice. The drink belongs to the broader 'aguas frescas' family — a category of lightly sweetened, water-based fruit, flower, or herb drinks that are ubiquitous at Mexican markets, taquerias, and restaurants. The preparation involves steeping or briefly blending fresh mint with water, straining the mixture, adding sugar and lime juice to taste, and serving chilled over ice. The result is a pale green, refreshing drink with a cool, herbaceous flavour, mild sweetness, and a bright citrus finish. Cucumber is sometimes added for additional freshness. Nutritionally, mint agua fresca is a low-calorie beverage (~18 kcal per 100g) with a modest carbohydrate content (~4.5g per 100g) derived primarily from added sugar. It is virtually fat-free and contains only trace protein. The sodium content is very low (~3 mg per 100g), making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low GI), though the added sugar means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size. Mint contributes minor amounts of vitamin A, vitamin C, and potassium. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. In Singapore, it is served at Mexican restaurants such as Fiesta Mexico and is priced at approximately SGD 10 per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mint agua fresca?
Is mint agua fresca good for diabetics?
What is agua fresca made of?
Is mint agua fresca gluten free?

confidence_reason: Mint agua fresca is not present as a standardised entry in any primary regional food composition database (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for water, mint, lime juice, and sugar in proportions consistent with standard agua fresca recipes. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable lightly sweetened beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — mint agua fresca is a water-based herb infusion with no fat-containing ingredients in meaningful quantities; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component analysis. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are 0.0: naturally zero for the same reason. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based beverage; B12 is not present in plant foods. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — no vitamin D source in ingredients. 6. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are -1.0: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for estimation at the dilution levels present in this beverage; values are below reliable detection threshold or not available in the source. 7. GI set to 40 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mint agua fresca exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 8. glycemic_index set to 40 (low GI ≤55 threshold); gi_category is low. 9. Serving size of 300g (300 ml) is based on typical restaurant glass portion for aguas frescas. 10. Price of SGD 10.00 is sourced from field survey data listing for Fiesta Mexico - Three Amigos Indian and Mexican Restaurant (input record); price data is not used in nutritional scoring. 11. Local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as descriptive translations appropriate for a Mexican beverage served in Singapore; these are not traditional names in those languages but functional translations for multilingual retrieval.
